title: Sts. Peter & Paul the Pillars — The Synaxis of the Twelve Apostles
narrative:
title": "Sts. Peter & Paul the Pillars — The Synaxis of the Twelve Apostles

narrative": "The Great Feast of the Holy, Glorious, and Praiseworthy Apostles Peter and Paul the Pillars of the Church, and the Synaxis of the Holy Twelve Apostles

On the thirtieth of Sene, the holy Church closes the tenth month with the Great Feast of the holy, glorious, and praiseworthy leaders of the Apostles, Peter and Paul — the Pillars of the Church, the Princes of the Apostolic College, the fisherman of Galilee and the Pharisee of Tarsus who between them carried the Gospel of the Resurrection from the shores of the Sea of Galilee to the heart of the Roman Empire and who sealed their witness with their blood in Rome under the Emperor Nero, the fisherman crucified upside down on the Vatican hill and the tentmaker beheaded on the Ostian Way. This feast is one of the greatest in the entire liturgical year of the Tewahedo church, a feast that brings the month of Sene — the month most saturated with apostolic witnesses — to its triumphant conclusion with the celebration of the two men who are, in the tradition's understanding, the primary human channels through which the apostolic faith was transmitted from Jerusalem to the world.

The theology of Peter in the Tewahedo tradition is a theology of the restored sinner: Peter is the apostle who denied the Lord three times in the courtyard of the high priest, who went out and wept bitterly, and who was then restored by the risen Christ in the threefold 'Do you love me?' on the shore of the Sea of Galilee — a restoration that not only cancelled the triple denial but deepened the apostolic commission, making Peter's love the foundation of his pastoral charge: 'Feed my lambs, tend my sheep, feed my sheep.' Peter is the tradition's patron of all those whose faith has been shaken and restored, all those who have denied by their actions if not their words and who have found in the risen Christ not a judge but a questioner whose question — 'Do you love me?' — is the most merciful and the most demanding that can be put to a human soul. His martyrdom in Rome, requesting crucifixion upside down as unworthy to die in the same posture as his Lord, is the final expression of the humility that his restoration had completed in him: the man who once denied is the man who finally offers everything, and offers it in the most self-abasing way he can conceive.

The theology of Paul in the Tewahedo tradition is a theology of the transformed opponent: Paul is the apostle who persecuted the Church before he joined it, who held the coats of those who stoned Stephen, who travelled to Damascus with letters authorising him to arrest Christians, and who was struck blind on the road by the risen Christ and heard the question 'Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me?' — the question that revealed to him, in a single blinding flash, that to persecute the Church was to persecute Christ, that the body of believers he had been hunting was the body of the one who was now addressing him from the light. Paul's conversion is the tradition's supreme instance of the divine irony that makes persecutors into apostles, opponents into advocates, enemies into saints, and the fourteen letters attributed to him — the most sustained body of theological reflection in the New Testament — are the intellectual fruit of a mind that had been formed to oppose the Gospel and was then, by the grace of the encounter on the road, turned entirely and irrevocably in its service.

The Synaxis of the Holy Twelve Apostles, celebrated on this same thirtieth day of Sene, gathers around the feast of the apostolic princes the full company of the Twelve: the college that Jesus constituted in the number of the tribes of Israel, whose names are inscribed on the twelve foundations of the heavenly city in the Book of Revelation, whose mission to the whole world was the primary means by which the Resurrection was proclaimed to every nation. The Synaxis is the tradition's way of ending the month not with two saints, however great, but with the full apostolic body — Peter and Paul and all their brothers, the fishermen and the tax collectors and the zealot and the doubter and the betrayed commission of the one who replaced the betrayer — gathered into a single eucharistic assembly that is itself the image of the Church: the community constituted by the apostolic proclamation, ordered around the apostolic succession, living within the apostolic faith, and moving toward the apostolic destination, which is the city whose twelve foundations bear their twelve names. The thirtieth of Sene, last day of the month that is most saturated with the apostolic spirit, closes not with an ending but with a gathering: all the apostles present, all the witnesses of the month assembled, the whole company of heaven in communion with the whole company of earth, the feast that anticipates the feast that will have no end. Amen.
